1: <header>Life cycle environmental impacts of five technological routes for straw utilisation in China </header>
Xiang Li, Yueying Du, Huimin Chang, Jing Ai, Yan Zhao School of Environment, Beijing Normal University, Beijing, 100875, China

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Straw utilisation Material flow Life cycle assessment Environmental impacts Technological comparison|Whilst five technological routes have been proposed in China and implemented for the comprehensive utilisation of crop straw, as fertilizer, fodder, fuel, basic material, and raw material, respectively, there is still uncertainty regarding their efficiency and environmental performance. This study compared the material flows, environ- mental impacts, and potential improvements of these five routes using full-scale data in the central region of China. All routes achieved efficient utilisation in terms of total solid with transfer coefficients of 67%~100%, but the fertiliser, fuel and raw material routes required significant amounts of manure and wood chips, respectively, due to the process requirements. On the other hand, the routes producing briquette fuel and board had lower overall loads to the mid-point environmental categories than the others, with product substitutions leading to the highest savings in terms of climate change (-1330 kg CO2-eq/t and -311 kg CO2-eq/t, respectively). The fer- tiliser route performed differently in terms of toxic and non-toxic impact categories, with use-on-land of compost contributing -162 kg CO2-eq/t savings to climate change impacts by carbon sequestration and fertilizer sub- stitution, and at the same time introducing N, P and trace metal pollution. According to the parameter sensitivity analysis, even slight reductions in material and energy consumption could lead to varying degrees of improve- ment in carbon reduction. Overall, the findings of this study provide quantitative evidence for the selection of appropriate technologies and the sustainable utilisation of crop straw, by maximizing the benefits of crop straw utilisation while minimizing its negative effects to the environment.
2: Agricultural residues, also known as agro-residues or crop-residues, are harvest wastes produced during harvesting of agricultural crops, which can be classified as primary residues such as stalk and straw left in the field after harvesting and secondary residues that remain after processing into a valuable resource [1]. Among these agricultural resi- dues, crop stalk/straw with a global amount of approximately 3 billion tons per year accounts for the majority of generation world widely and is also an important biomass resource with significant utilisation potential [2]. Statistics show that the production of crop straw in China reached 0.9 X 109 t in 2015 [3], whilst that of wheat straw reached 1.53 X 108 t in 2017 [4]. Due to the large yield and dispersed distribution of crop straw, its collection, transportation and recycling involve high costs, which hinder its centralised treatment [5]. In addition, the Chinese government aimed to utilize at least 85% of crop straws nationally by 2020, but the rest of crop straw were still being discarded or burned in open air or as household energy [6], thereby resulting in both resource

wastage and air pollution. Burning 1 ton of straw in an open field can release 1168 kg CO2, 1.0 kg CH4 and 0.06 kg N2O of greenhouse gases on average and emit significant amounts of particulate matter [7]. In addition, the open burning of crop straw evaporates soil moisture and destroys the organic matter present in the soil [8]. Therefore, suitable and sustainable approaches for utilising crop straw have received much attention from agricultural, environmental and economic aspects. To promote the comprehensive utilisation of crop straw, the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs of the People's Republic of China has recommended five different routes for the reference of relevant sectors [9]. These routes include utilising straw as a fertiliser, fodder, fuel, basic material and raw material [10]; these routes cover most uses of straw and encourages the integration of various approaches according to local conditions to maximise the utilisation potential of this material [5,11]. For example, basic material aims to provide growth conditions for ani- mals, plants or microorganisms, such as growth medium for fungi cultivation; while raw material aims to provide feedstocks for industry or manufacturing to produce goods. However, local sectors and
companies are still facing problems in technological comparison and selection and in balancing the environmental and economic costs with the benefits.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) offers a standardised tool for comparing different technological routes from the environmental perspective [11, 12]. LCA has also been widely used in solid waste technologies and systems, including those for municipal solid waste [13-15], industrial waste [16] and agricultural waste [17]. Different methods for straw utilisation were also studied using LCA to reveal their environmental performance [18,19]. For example, Cheng et al. [20] compared the environmental impact potentials of power generation, ethanol produc- tion and fodder production from wheat straw and revealed that ethanol production for fuel provided great benefits to human health, ecological environment, climate change and natural resources. Soam et al. [7] re- ported that generating electricity and producing biogas from 1 ton of straw contributed 1471 kg CO2-eq and 1023 kg CO2-eq to global warming potentials, respectively. Sun et al. [5] evaluated the environ- mental benefits of utilising straw via pulping and papermaking, whereas Zhao et al. [14,21] systematically compared different approaches for converting corn straw to bioethanol via material flow analysis (MFA) and LCA enhanced by large data analysis. Previous studies clearly

indicate that the environmental benefits of straw utilisation come from the substitution of other materials, fuels or energy [22-24]. However, many studies have used experimental or literature data for impact assessment and differed in their goals, scopes and methods. Accordingly, the technological routes for straw utilisation that are the most envi- ronment friendly and promising in practice remain unclear.

To reveal and compare the environmental performance of the five routes for straw utilisation (as fertilizer, fodder, fuel, basic material, and raw material, respectively), this study collected practical input and output data on materials and energy by performing field investigations on five full-scale facilities with one for each corresponding technology. Using these data, this study established the material flows of the five routes and applied comprehensive LCA to evaluate their contributions to carbon footprints and other environmental impact categories. Sensi- tivity analysis on operational parameters was also performed to reveal the improvement potentials of each technological route in terms of environmental impact. The findings are supposed to provide quantita- tively guide future technological comparisons of crop straw utilisation from the environmental perspective.

6: Agricultural residues, also known as agro-residues or crop-residues, are harvest wastes produced during harvesting of agricultural crops, which can be classified as primary residues such as stalk and straw left in the field after harvesting and secondary residues that remain after processing into a valuable resource [1]. Among these agricultural resi- dues, crop stalk/straw with a global amount of approximately 3 billion tons per year accounts for the majority of generation world widely and is also an important biomass resource with significant utilisation potential [2]. Statistics show that the production of crop straw in China reached 0.9 X 109 t in 2015 [3], whilst that of wheat straw reached 1.53 X 108 t in 2017 [4]. Due to the large yield and dispersed distribution of crop straw, its collection, transportation and recycling involve high costs, which hinder its centralised treatment [5]. In addition, the Chinese government aimed to utilize at least 85% of crop straws nationally by 2020, but the rest of crop straw were still being discarded or burned in open air or as household energy [6], thereby resulting in both resource

wastage and air pollution. Burning 1 ton of straw in an open field can release 1168 kg CO2, 1.0 kg CH4 and 0.06 kg N2O of greenhouse gases on average and emit significant amounts of particulate matter [7]. In addition, the open burning of crop straw evaporates soil moisture and destroys the organic matter present in the soil [8]. Therefore, suitable and sustainable approaches for utilising crop straw have received much attention from agricultural, environmental and economic aspects. To promote the comprehensive utilisation of crop straw, the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs of the People's Republic of China has recommended five different routes for the reference of relevant sectors [9]. These routes include utilising straw as a fertiliser, fodder, fuel, basic material and raw material [10]; these routes cover most uses of straw and encourages the integration of various approaches according to local conditions to maximise the utilisation potential of this material [5,11]. For example, basic material aims to provide growth conditions for ani- mals, plants or microorganisms, such as growth medium for fungi cultivation; while raw material aims to provide feedstocks for industry or manufacturing to produce goods. However, local sectors and
companies are still facing problems in technological comparison and selection and in balancing the environmental and economic costs with the benefits.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) offers a standardised tool for comparing different technological routes from the environmental perspective [11, 12]. LCA has also been widely used in solid waste technologies and systems, including those for municipal solid waste [13-15], industrial waste [16] and agricultural waste [17]. Different methods for straw utilisation were also studied using LCA to reveal their environmental performance [18,19]. For example, Cheng et al. [20] compared the environmental impact potentials of power generation, ethanol produc- tion and fodder production from wheat straw and revealed that ethanol production for fuel provided great benefits to human health, ecological environment, climate change and natural resources. Soam et al. [7] re- ported that generating electricity and producing biogas from 1 ton of straw contributed 1471 kg CO2-eq and 1023 kg CO2-eq to global warming potentials, respectively. Sun et al. [5] evaluated the environ- mental benefits of utilising straw via pulping and papermaking, whereas Zhao et al. [14,21] systematically compared different approaches for converting corn straw to bioethanol via material flow analysis (MFA) and LCA enhanced by large data analysis. Previous studies clearly

indicate that the environmental benefits of straw utilisation come from the substitution of other materials, fuels or energy [22-24]. However, many studies have used experimental or literature data for impact assessment and differed in their goals, scopes and methods. Accordingly, the technological routes for straw utilisation that are the most envi- ronment friendly and promising in practice remain unclear.

To reveal and compare the environmental performance of the five routes for straw utilisation (as fertilizer, fodder, fuel, basic material, and raw material, respectively), this study collected practical input and output data on materials and energy by performing field investigations on five full-scale facilities with one for each corresponding technology. Using these data, this study established the material flows of the five routes and applied comprehensive LCA to evaluate their contributions to carbon footprints and other environmental impact categories. Sensi- tivity analysis on operational parameters was also performed to reveal the improvement potentials of each technological route in terms of environmental impact. The findings are supposed to provide quantita- tively guide future technological comparisons of crop straw utilisation from the environmental perspective.

10: Agricultural residues, also known as agro-residues or crop-residues, are harvest wastes produced during harvesting of agricultural crops, which can be classified as primary residues such as stalk and straw left in the field after harvesting and secondary residues that remain after processing into a valuable resource [1]. Among these agricultural resi- dues, crop stalk/straw with a global amount of approximately 3 billion tons per year accounts for the majority of generation world widely and is also an important biomass resource with significant utilisation potential [2]. Statistics show that the production of crop straw in China reached 0.9 X 109 t in 2015 [3], whilst that of wheat straw reached 1.53 X 108 t in 2017 [4]. Due to the large yield and dispersed distribution of crop straw, its collection, transportation and recycling involve high costs, which hinder its centralised treatment [5]. In addition, the Chinese government aimed to utilize at least 85% of crop straws nationally by 2020, but the rest of crop straw were still being discarded or burned in open air or as household energy [6], thereby resulting in both resource

wastage and air pollution. Burning 1 ton of straw in an open field can release 1168 kg CO2, 1.0 kg CH4 and 0.06 kg N2O of greenhouse gases on average and emit significant amounts of particulate matter [7]. In addition, the open burning of crop straw evaporates soil moisture and destroys the organic matter present in the soil [8]. Therefore, suitable and sustainable approaches for utilising crop straw have received much attention from agricultural, environmental and economic aspects. To promote the comprehensive utilisation of crop straw, the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs of the People's Republic of China has recommended five different routes for the reference of relevant sectors [9]. These routes include utilising straw as a fertiliser, fodder, fuel, basic material and raw material [10]; these routes cover most uses of straw and encourages the integration of various approaches according to local conditions to maximise the utilisation potential of this material [5,11]. For example, basic material aims to provide growth conditions for ani- mals, plants or microorganisms, such as growth medium for fungi cultivation; while raw material aims to provide feedstocks for industry or manufacturing to produce goods. However, local sectors and
companies are still facing problems in technological comparison and selection and in balancing the environmental and economic costs with the benefits.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) offers a standardised tool for comparing different technological routes from the environmental perspective [11, 12]. LCA has also been widely used in solid waste technologies and systems, including those for municipal solid waste [13-15], industrial waste [16] and agricultural waste [17]. Different methods for straw utilisation were also studied using LCA to reveal their environmental performance [18,19]. For example, Cheng et al. [20] compared the environmental impact potentials of power generation, ethanol produc- tion and fodder production from wheat straw and revealed that ethanol production for fuel provided great benefits to human health, ecological environment, climate change and natural resources. Soam et al. [7] re- ported that generating electricity and producing biogas from 1 ton of straw contributed 1471 kg CO2-eq and 1023 kg CO2-eq to global warming potentials, respectively. Sun et al. [5] evaluated the environ- mental benefits of utilising straw via pulping and papermaking, whereas Zhao et al. [14,21] systematically compared different approaches for converting corn straw to bioethanol via material flow analysis (MFA) and LCA enhanced by large data analysis. Previous studies clearly

indicate that the environmental benefits of straw utilisation come from the substitution of other materials, fuels or energy [22-24]. However, many studies have used experimental or literature data for impact assessment and differed in their goals, scopes and methods. Accordingly, the technological routes for straw utilisation that are the most envi- ronment friendly and promising in practice remain unclear.

To reveal and compare the environmental performance of the five routes for straw utilisation (as fertilizer, fodder, fuel, basic material, and raw material, respectively), this study collected practical input and output data on materials and energy by performing field investigations on five full-scale facilities with one for each corresponding technology. Using these data, this study established the material flows of the five routes and applied comprehensive LCA to evaluate their contributions to carbon footprints and other environmental impact categories. Sensi- tivity analysis on operational parameters was also performed to reveal the improvement potentials of each technological route in terms of environmental impact. The findings are supposed to provide quantita- tively guide future technological comparisons of crop straw utilisation from the environmental perspective.

11: According to the five routes for straw utilisation, five full-scale companies that are located in the same city in the central region of China and apply different routes for wheat straw utilisation were selected as typical practical technologies.

In this city 1.1 Mt of the crop straw was generated annually and approximately 1.0 Mt was utilized with the five routes. Among them, 65% of the crop straw was used in the fertiliser route, 8% in the fuel route, 12% in the raw material route, and the fodder route and basic material route shared the rest 15%. Therefore, the five alternative routes coexisted and were comparable in terms of dealing with the same crop straw. Five technological scenarios were then established for the MFA and LCA based on the same quantity of wheat straw, from its input to the technologies to its output to the society, as illustrated in Fig. 1 in Section 3.1 along with the material flow results. The results of the five scenarios were further compared, without assigning a baseline scenario.

Scenario 1 (S1): Fertiliser route. After collection and transportation, the straw is smashed, and a portion of the product is sent for anaerobic digestion (AD) with manure. Meanwhile, the remaining part is sent for composting with the digestate from AD after solid-liquid separation. The major products in this scenario include biogas for heat generation in farmers' individual houses, as well as compost and liquid fertiliser which can substitute for chemical fertiliser.

Scenario 2 (S2): Fodder route. After collection and transportation, the straw is smashed and packed for sale as fodder. This fodder serves as the product in this scenario, which can substitute for commercial feed.

Scenario 3 (S3): Fuel route. After collection and transportation, the straw is smashed, naturally dried (without external energy consump- tion) and squeezed to form biomass briquettes. The product in this scenario is briquette fuel, which can substitute coal by being used to generate energy via combustion.

Scenario 4 (S4): Basic material route. After collection and trans- portation, the straw is smashed and fermented by adding water. The fermented straw is then sterilised and inoculated for growing mush- room. The product in this scenario is the nutrition medium, which can substitute for the nutrients present in fertiliser.

Scenario 5 (S5): Raw material route. After collection and trans- portation, the straw is dried, smashed and manufactured via a hot- pressing process to produce board. This board serves as the product in this scenario, which can substitute for medium-density fibre boards.

14: The above scenarios were compared using input and output data and material flows that are consistent with practical technologies. The data on the main material flows, including the input and output of each sub- process, were collected via field investigations of five full-scale com- panies. The transfer coefficients of the materials used in the processes were calculated using these input and output data. Data on the material, chemical and energy consumption that drive the processes were also obtained from full-scale operational data. Given the different scales of the companies under investigation, all data were normalised into 1 ton of wheat straw treated by ignoring the possible effects caused by the scale difference. The detailed input and output data of the five techno- logical scenarios in terms of 1 ton of straw are available in the Appendix.

The functional unit of the LCA was defined as the utilisation of 1 ton (1000 kg wet weight) of wheat straw with the same quality by applying the five routes mentioned above. The system boundary included the collection, transportation and all major processes of the five techno- logical routes, including the use of materials and energy, the treatment of solid and liquid residues and the downstream use of the recovered materials, fuel and energy from wheat straw, if applicable. System expansion was applied following the key LCA principles. Specifically,

the alternative products that were avoided during the material and en- ergy production were subtracted: the compost substitutes commercial fertiliser in terms of the nutrient N and P; the fodder substitutes silage in terms of the mass; the briquette fuel substitutes coal in terms of heating value; the nutrition medium substitutes commercial fertiliser in terms of the nutrient N and P; and the board substitutes medium density fibre- board in terms of volume. To follow the framework of waste-oriented LCA and ensure a consistent and concise system boundary, the wheat straw and supplementary materials (e.g. manure and waste wood chips) were assumed to be available as waste materials without any current use and environmental rucksack. The LCA and the corresponding environ- mental impacts do not involve the contributions of supplementary waste materials to ensure consistency in both functional unit and system boundary, and only consider the contribution of wheat straw. The alternative uses of wheat straw, soil carbon loss or direct land use changes resulting from the use of the wheat straw were also ignored.

According to the average analysis data, the composition of the wheat straw was set as follows (Table 1): water content, 28% of wet weight; total solid (TS), 72% of wet weight; volatile solids (VS), 94.65% of TS; biodegradable carbon, 31% of TS. The detailed element composition of the wheat straw according to Vassilev et al. [25] can be found in the Appendix. This composition was kept consistent throughout the study across the five technological scenarios.

18: <header>2.3. Modelling tool and method for life cycle impact assessment </header>

The LCA of the technological scenarios was performed using EASE- TECH, a specialised LCA model that assesses the environmental tech- nologies of complex systems handling heterogeneous material flows (particularly solid waste) via process modelling [26]. The modelling was carried out for the five scenarios with system expansion. Apart from the data on main material flows collected from the field investigation, the life cycle inventory data for external processes, including the production of chemicals, heat, electricity and other materials, were obtained from the Ecoinvent database (V3.8, allocation at the point of substitution; htt ps://ecoinvent.org/) and were kept consistent across the different scenarios.

Following the recommendations specified in the International Reference Life Cycle Data System (ILCD) Handbook, the LCA method adopted in this study included the following impact categories [27]: climate change (global warming potential or GWP), stratospheric ozone depletion (OD), acidification (AD), eutrophication (terrestrial) (EPt), eutrophication (freshwater) (EPf), eutrophication (marine) (EPm), photochemical ozone formation (POF), ecotoxicity (freshwater) (ET), human toxicity (cancer effects) (HTC), human toxicity (non-cancer ef- fects) (HTnc), particulate matter (PM) and ionising radiation (IR). The impact assessment results were normalised to personal equivalents by the annual impact from an average person according to the ISO 14040 standards to facilitate a comparison across categories. Due to the lack of information in specific regions like China, the normalisation factors of all impact categories also refer to the ILCD methods and are listed in the Appendix. Given the different confidence and recommendation levels for the impact categories based on the quality of the characterisation model, the results are divided into toxic and non-toxic categories and the climate change impact was emphasised during the discussion.
Composition of the wheat straw applied in the five technological scenarios.
Water content (% wet weight)|Total solid (TS) (% wet weight)|Volatile solids (VS) (% TS)|Biodegradable carbon (% TS) 28|72|94.65|31
2.4. Sensitivity analysis and optimisation potential calculation
The sensitivity analysis was performed using the selected parameters for each route, and the results were expressed via the sensitivity ratio (SR) calculated for each parameter as shown in Eq. (1).
A result initial result SR Aparameter initial parameter

During the analysis, one parameter was decreased by 5% to stay within a pseudo-linear range, and all other parameters were fixed at their initial values. For consistent illustration, results were expressed as relative sensitivity ratios by normalizing the SR values into percentages of the highest SR in each route.

The selected parameters were assumed optimised by changing 10% of their initial values, and the environmental impacts of the optimised scenarios were compared with the initial values to reveal the environ- mental performance improvement potential of each route.

21: The material flows of the five routes in terms of 1000 kg wheat straw (wet weight) are shown in Fig. 1. The S2 and S4 routes only used wheat straw as raw materials, whereas the other routes required large amounts of additional materials, including sewage and manure (S1) and waste wood materials (S3 and S5).

In S1, 714 kg out of the 1000 kg straw was used for biogas production after collection and transportation. Meanwhile, 4428 kg of sewage was added in the pre-treatment process, and only 25.2 kg of biogas was produced after anaerobic digestion probably due to the low degradation of wheat straw and the low organic content of sewage. The digestate was subjected to a solid-liquid separation, 992.5 kg of liquid fertiliser was produced via the concentration of the liquid phase and 142.8 kg of solid residues were routed to composting processes. In addition, 286 kg of wheat straw, 142.8 kg of solid residues and 2857 kg of livestock manure were composted to produce 1685.6 kg of fertiliser with 48.7% mass loss due to organic degradation and water evaporation. The compost was mainly derived from manure, and the contribution of wheat straw was calculated in terms of its TS. The conversion efficient of biogas from wheat straw was as low as 0.4%, whereas that of liquid fertiliser was 56.8%. Approximately 42.1% of the wheat straw flowed to the compost and contributed to 25% of its mass.
In S2, 1000 kg of wheat straw was collected, transported, smashed and packaged as fodder without any material loss or supplement.

In S3, the wheat straw was used as a secondary material to produce briquette fuel. Specifically, 1000 kg wheat straw material was added to 6666.7 kg of waste wood chips after collection, transportation and smashing, which resulted in 153.3 kg of solid waste. After losing 1472.6 kg of water in the drying process via evaporation, the final product weighed 6040.74 kg. In terms of TS content, 85.8% of the wheat straw was converted into fuel, which contributed to 14.2% of the briquette fuel.

In S4, 905.66 kg out of 1000 kg of wheat straw was smashed, and 887.55 kg was fermented, packaged and sterilised as a nutrition medium for mushroom growth. The solid residue in this scenario accounted for 11.2% of the total and was sent to a waste incinerator.

In S5, 242.4 kg out of 1000 kg of wheat straw was lost in the drying process due to water evaporation, and 22.6 kg was lost in the smashing process as dust, impurities and waste. With additional 893.3 kg of waste wood materials, the smashed wheat straw was processed via forming, hot pressing and trimming to produce 1488.6 kg of board products, with additional 0.2 kg loss. The overall mass loss was 242.6 kg, which can be

attributed to moisture, dust and VOCs, and additional 162.3 kg of solid residue was generated. In terms of TS content, the wheat straw accounted for 45.1% of the board, which corresponded to 67.2% of the wheat straw converted into products.

The five routes had wheat straw transfer coefficients ranging from 67% to 100% in terms of wet weight. However, considering the 28% water content of raw wheat straw, the transfer coefficients in terms of TS reached as high as 93%-100% in the five routes. Therefore, these routes can achieve an efficient utilisation of wheat straw, except for those routes that utilize significant amounts of other waste materials, such as wood chips and manure.

25: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts and process contributions of the five routes </header>

LCA was performed in terms of 1 ton of wheat straw (wet weight) without considering the contributions of supplementary waste mate- rials, such as wood chips or manure, to ensure comparability. Fig. 2 shows the overall normalised environmental impacts of the five routes to 12 categories [28]. In general, the environmental impacts to the non-toxic categories were relatively low, whereas those to the toxic categories (ET, HTn and HTnc) were relatively high, mainly because the normalisation references of the toxic categories are quite low in the LCIA method (details in the Appendix). The results for these two categories were thus discussed separately. Scenarios S1 and S4 showed similar trends in their normalised impacts, resulting in minor savings to CC and EPf (-0.01 to -0.25 PE), minor loads to AD, EPt and EPm (0.01-0.04 PE) and significant loads to HTnc (6.0-6.8 PE) By contrast, scenarios S3 and S5 resulted in high savings to CC (-0.03 to -0.18 PM (-0.15 to 0.20 PE) and HTc (-0.2 to -0.4 PE) whereas S2 resulted in high savings to EPt and EPm (0.03-0.04 P In terms of overall impact, S3 and S5 outperformed all the other routes. The detailed process contributions to several typical categories (CC, POF, AD, EPt, ET and HTnc) are shown in Fig. 3 in terms of characterised impacts to explain the abovementioned results. The results for the other categories are available in the Appendix.

In S1, the transportation, solid/liquid separation, wastewater treat- ment and composting processes contributed loads to all categories, including CC, POF, AD and EPt, due to fuel and energy consumption. By contrast, the use-on-land of compost contributed some savings to CC (-162 kg CO2-eq/t) mainly due to the sequestration of undegraded biogenic carbon (approximately -90 kg CO2-eq/t) and the avoidance of

Fig. 2. Overall normalised environmental impacts to the non-toxic and toxic categories of the five scenarios processing 1000 kg of wheat straw. CC: climate change, OD: stratospheric ozone depletion, POF: photochemical ozone forma- tion, AD: acidification, EPt: eutrophication (terrestrial), EPf: eutrophication (freshwater), EPm: eutrophication (marine), PM: particulate matter, IR: ionis- ing radiation, ET: ecotoxicity (freshwater), HTc: human toxicity (cancer effects) and HTnc: human toxicity (non-cancer effects).

Fig. 3. Process contribution of the five scenarios to the characterized impacts. a) Climate change (CC); b) photochemical ozone formation (POF); c) acidification (AD); d) eutrophication (terrestrial) (EPt); e) ecotoxicity (freshwater) (ET); and f) human toxicity (non-cancer effects) (HTnc).

fertiliser utilisation (approximately -60 kg CO2-eq/t). However, the use-on-land of compost had some impact potentials to AD and EPt due to the loss of N and P to surface water during compost utilisation. Detailed data on use-on-land of compost for the substitution of fertilizer are available in the Appendix. Meanwhile, the impact potentials to ET and HTnc can be ascribed to the presence of trace metal elements in the compost.

In S2, the energy consumption of the smashing, transportation and packaging processes contributed some loads to all impact categories. By contrast, using straw as fodder avoided the impact potentials to CC, POF, AD and EPt by substituting the same amount of silage, but the possible different effects on the livestock were not considered due to lack of data. This ideal assumption may result in uncertainty in the substitution ratio of silage and further affect the impact potentials to certain degrees.

In S3, fuel substitution contributed the largest savings to all impact categories. Specifically, using wheat straw as biomass fuel can avoid significant fossil CO2 emissions (-1330 kg CO2-eq/t) compared with traditional fuels, such as coal. The incineration of residues also resulted
in small savings due to energy recovery and substitution (-16 kg CO2- eq/t). Transportation and smashing contributed some loads to all impact categories due to fuel and energy consumption.

In S4, the incineration of residues contributed some savings to all categories due to the energy recovery from biomass. By contrast, nutrient substitution was beneficial to reducing CC impact (-143 kg CO2-eq/t) due to carbon sequestration and fertiliser avoidance yet resulted in loads to AD and EPt due to nutrient loss and to ET and HTnc due to trace elements. However, these results are uncertain in quanti- fication due to the lack of information about the behaviour of nutrient and metal elements in growing mushroom (the behaviour in compost utilisation was thus considered). Apart from transportation, smashing and fermentation also consumed electricity and heat, thereby contrib- uting slight loads to all categories.

In S5, the produced board, which substituted for boards made from conventional materials, contributed the largest savings to CC (-311 kg CO2-eq/t) and all other impact categories. Additional 15%-25% savings were obtained from the incineration of residues by recovering energy

from the heating value of wheat straw. However, the forming and pressing processes contributed significant loads to all impact categories, counteracting approximately 25% of the non-toxic savings and even 50% of the toxic savings, mainly due to the additive tackiness agent (polymeric diphenyl methane diisocyanate), whose production causes significant impact potentials though the amount is normally low.

Regarding to the climate change impacts, the fuel route (S3) showed the highest saving of 1300 kg CO2-eq/t in all the five scenarios, and the fodder route obtained the lowest saving due to the substitute value of silage. This is in accordance with the results obtained by Soam et al. though the system boundaries are slightly different, which revealed climate change savings of 1471 kg CO2-eq/t and 185 kg CO2-eq/t in for recovering energy and fodder from wheat straw, respectively [29]. The scenarios S1, S4 and S5 also achieved moderate savings to climate change impacts, given the benefits from product substitution. However, fuel or energy recovery from wheat straw is currently an important approach to reduce the climate change impacts as demonstrated by some other studies [30,31], especially under the fossil-based fuel or energy system.
26: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts and process contributions of the five routes </header>

LCA was performed in terms of 1 ton of wheat straw (wet weight) without considering the contributions of supplementary waste mate- rials, such as wood chips or manure, to ensure comparability. Fig. 2 shows the overall normalised environmental impacts of the five routes to 12 categories [28]. In general, the environmental impacts to the non-toxic categories were relatively low, whereas those to the toxic categories (ET, HTn and HTnc) were relatively high, mainly because the normalisation references of the toxic categories are quite low in the LCIA method (details in the Appendix). The results for these two categories were thus discussed separately. Scenarios S1 and S4 showed similar trends in their normalised impacts, resulting in minor savings to CC and EPf (-0.01 to -0.25 PE), minor loads to AD, EPt and EPm (0.01-0.04 PE) and significant loads to HTnc (6.0-6.8 PE) By contrast, scenarios S3 and S5 resulted in high savings to CC (-0.03 to -0.18 PM (-0.15 to 0.20 PE) and HTc (-0.2 to -0.4 PE) whereas S2 resulted in high savings to EPt and EPm (0.03-0.04 P In terms of overall impact, S3 and S5 outperformed all the other routes. The detailed process contributions to several typical categories (CC, POF, AD, EPt, ET and HTnc) are shown in Fig. 3 in terms of characterised impacts to explain the abovementioned results. The results for the other categories are available in the Appendix.

In S1, the transportation, solid/liquid separation, wastewater treat- ment and composting processes contributed loads to all categories, including CC, POF, AD and EPt, due to fuel and energy consumption. By contrast, the use-on-land of compost contributed some savings to CC (-162 kg CO2-eq/t) mainly due to the sequestration of undegraded biogenic carbon (approximately -90 kg CO2-eq/t) and the avoidance of

Fig. 3. Process contribution of the five scenarios to the characterized impacts. a) Climate change (CC); b) photochemical ozone formation (POF); c) acidification (AD); d) eutrophication (terrestrial) (EPt); e) ecotoxicity (freshwater) (ET); and f) human toxicity (non-cancer effects) (HTnc).

fertiliser utilisation (approximately -60 kg CO2-eq/t). However, the use-on-land of compost had some impact potentials to AD and EPt due to the loss of N and P to surface water during compost utilisation. Detailed data on use-on-land of compost for the substitution of fertilizer are available in the Appendix. Meanwhile, the impact potentials to ET and HTnc can be ascribed to the presence of trace metal elements in the compost.

In S2, the energy consumption of the smashing, transportation and packaging processes contributed some loads to all impact categories. By contrast, using straw as fodder avoided the impact potentials to CC, POF, AD and EPt by substituting the same amount of silage, but the possible different effects on the livestock were not considered due to lack of data. This ideal assumption may result in uncertainty in the substitution ratio of silage and further affect the impact potentials to certain degrees.

In S3, fuel substitution contributed the largest savings to all impact categories. Specifically, using wheat straw as biomass fuel can avoid significant fossil CO2 emissions (-1330 kg CO2-eq/t) compared with traditional fuels, such as coal. The incineration of residues also resulted
in small savings due to energy recovery and substitution (-16 kg CO2- eq/t). Transportation and smashing contributed some loads to all impact categories due to fuel and energy consumption.

In S4, the incineration of residues contributed some savings to all categories due to the energy recovery from biomass. By contrast, nutrient substitution was beneficial to reducing CC impact (-143 kg CO2-eq/t) due to carbon sequestration and fertiliser avoidance yet resulted in loads to AD and EPt due to nutrient loss and to ET and HTnc due to trace elements. However, these results are uncertain in quanti- fication due to the lack of information about the behaviour of nutrient and metal elements in growing mushroom (the behaviour in compost utilisation was thus considered). Apart from transportation, smashing and fermentation also consumed electricity and heat, thereby contrib- uting slight loads to all categories.

In S5, the produced board, which substituted for boards made from conventional materials, contributed the largest savings to CC (-311 kg CO2-eq/t) and all other impact categories. Additional 15%-25% savings were obtained from the incineration of residues by recovering energy

from the heating value of wheat straw. However, the forming and pressing processes contributed significant loads to all impact categories, counteracting approximately 25% of the non-toxic savings and even 50% of the toxic savings, mainly due to the additive tackiness agent (polymeric diphenyl methane diisocyanate), whose production causes significant impact potentials though the amount is normally low.

Regarding to the climate change impacts, the fuel route (S3) showed the highest saving of 1300 kg CO2-eq/t in all the five scenarios, and the fodder route obtained the lowest saving due to the substitute value of silage. This is in accordance with the results obtained by Soam et al. though the system boundaries are slightly different, which revealed climate change savings of 1471 kg CO2-eq/t and 185 kg CO2-eq/t in for recovering energy and fodder from wheat straw, respectively [29]. The scenarios S1, S4 and S5 also achieved moderate savings to climate change impacts, given the benefits from product substitution. However, fuel or energy recovery from wheat straw is currently an important approach to reduce the climate change impacts as demonstrated by some other studies [30,31], especially under the fossil-based fuel or energy system.
28: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts and process contributions of the five routes </header>

LCA was performed in terms of 1 ton of wheat straw (wet weight) without considering the contributions of supplementary waste mate- rials, such as wood chips or manure, to ensure comparability. Fig. 2 shows the overall normalised environmental impacts of the five routes to 12 categories [28]. In general, the environmental impacts to the non-toxic categories were relatively low, whereas those to the toxic categories (ET, HTn and HTnc) were relatively high, mainly because the normalisation references of the toxic categories are quite low in the LCIA method (details in the Appendix). The results for these two categories were thus discussed separately. Scenarios S1 and S4 showed similar trends in their normalised impacts, resulting in minor savings to CC and EPf (-0.01 to -0.25 PE), minor loads to AD, EPt and EPm (0.01-0.04 PE) and significant loads to HTnc (6.0-6.8 PE) By contrast, scenarios S3 and S5 resulted in high savings to CC (-0.03 to -0.18 PM (-0.15 to 0.20 PE) and HTc (-0.2 to -0.4 PE) whereas S2 resulted in high savings to EPt and EPm (0.03-0.04 P In terms of overall impact, S3 and S5 outperformed all the other routes. The detailed process contributions to several typical categories (CC, POF, AD, EPt, ET and HTnc) are shown in Fig. 3 in terms of characterised impacts to explain the abovementioned results. The results for the other categories are available in the Appendix.

In S1, the transportation, solid/liquid separation, wastewater treat- ment and composting processes contributed loads to all categories, including CC, POF, AD and EPt, due to fuel and energy consumption. By contrast, the use-on-land of compost contributed some savings to CC (-162 kg CO2-eq/t) mainly due to the sequestration of undegraded biogenic carbon (approximately -90 kg CO2-eq/t) and the avoidance of

Fig. 3. Process contribution of the five scenarios to the characterized impacts. a) Climate change (CC); b) photochemical ozone formation (POF); c) acidification (AD); d) eutrophication (terrestrial) (EPt); e) ecotoxicity (freshwater) (ET); and f) human toxicity (non-cancer effects) (HTnc).

fertiliser utilisation (approximately -60 kg CO2-eq/t). However, the use-on-land of compost had some impact potentials to AD and EPt due to the loss of N and P to surface water during compost utilisation. Detailed data on use-on-land of compost for the substitution of fertilizer are available in the Appendix. Meanwhile, the impact potentials to ET and HTnc can be ascribed to the presence of trace metal elements in the compost.

In S2, the energy consumption of the smashing, transportation and packaging processes contributed some loads to all impact categories. By contrast, using straw as fodder avoided the impact potentials to CC, POF, AD and EPt by substituting the same amount of silage, but the possible different effects on the livestock were not considered due to lack of data. This ideal assumption may result in uncertainty in the substitution ratio of silage and further affect the impact potentials to certain degrees.

In S3, fuel substitution contributed the largest savings to all impact categories. Specifically, using wheat straw as biomass fuel can avoid significant fossil CO2 emissions (-1330 kg CO2-eq/t) compared with traditional fuels, such as coal. The incineration of residues also resulted
in small savings due to energy recovery and substitution (-16 kg CO2- eq/t). Transportation and smashing contributed some loads to all impact categories due to fuel and energy consumption.

In S4, the incineration of residues contributed some savings to all categories due to the energy recovery from biomass. By contrast, nutrient substitution was beneficial to reducing CC impact (-143 kg CO2-eq/t) due to carbon sequestration and fertiliser avoidance yet resulted in loads to AD and EPt due to nutrient loss and to ET and HTnc due to trace elements. However, these results are uncertain in quanti- fication due to the lack of information about the behaviour of nutrient and metal elements in growing mushroom (the behaviour in compost utilisation was thus considered). Apart from transportation, smashing and fermentation also consumed electricity and heat, thereby contrib- uting slight loads to all categories.

In S5, the produced board, which substituted for boards made from conventional materials, contributed the largest savings to CC (-311 kg CO2-eq/t) and all other impact categories. Additional 15%-25% savings were obtained from the incineration of residues by recovering energy

from the heating value of wheat straw. However, the forming and pressing processes contributed significant loads to all impact categories, counteracting approximately 25% of the non-toxic savings and even 50% of the toxic savings, mainly due to the additive tackiness agent (polymeric diphenyl methane diisocyanate), whose production causes significant impact potentials though the amount is normally low.

Regarding to the climate change impacts, the fuel route (S3) showed the highest saving of 1300 kg CO2-eq/t in all the five scenarios, and the fodder route obtained the lowest saving due to the substitute value of silage. This is in accordance with the results obtained by Soam et al. though the system boundaries are slightly different, which revealed climate change savings of 1471 kg CO2-eq/t and 185 kg CO2-eq/t in for recovering energy and fodder from wheat straw, respectively [29]. The scenarios S1, S4 and S5 also achieved moderate savings to climate change impacts, given the benefits from product substitution. However, fuel or energy recovery from wheat straw is currently an important approach to reduce the climate change impacts as demonstrated by some other studies [30,31], especially under the fossil-based fuel or energy system.
29: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts and process contributions of the five routes </header>

LCA was performed in terms of 1 ton of wheat straw (wet weight) without considering the contributions of supplementary waste mate- rials, such as wood chips or manure, to ensure comparability. Fig. 2 shows the overall normalised environmental impacts of the five routes to 12 categories [28]. In general, the environmental impacts to the non-toxic categories were relatively low, whereas those to the toxic categories (ET, HTn and HTnc) were relatively high, mainly because the normalisation references of the toxic categories are quite low in the LCIA method (details in the Appendix). The results for these two categories were thus discussed separately. Scenarios S1 and S4 showed similar trends in their normalised impacts, resulting in minor savings to CC and EPf (-0.01 to -0.25 PE), minor loads to AD, EPt and EPm (0.01-0.04 PE) and significant loads to HTnc (6.0-6.8 PE) By contrast, scenarios S3 and S5 resulted in high savings to CC (-0.03 to -0.18 PM (-0.15 to 0.20 PE) and HTc (-0.2 to -0.4 PE) whereas S2 resulted in high savings to EPt and EPm (0.03-0.04 P In terms of overall impact, S3 and S5 outperformed all the other routes. The detailed process contributions to several typical categories (CC, POF, AD, EPt, ET and HTnc) are shown in Fig. 3 in terms of characterised impacts to explain the abovementioned results. The results for the other categories are available in the Appendix.

In S1, the transportation, solid/liquid separation, wastewater treat- ment and composting processes contributed loads to all categories, including CC, POF, AD and EPt, due to fuel and energy consumption. By contrast, the use-on-land of compost contributed some savings to CC (-162 kg CO2-eq/t) mainly due to the sequestration of undegraded biogenic carbon (approximately -90 kg CO2-eq/t) and the avoidance of

30: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts and process contributions of the five routes </header>

LCA was performed in terms of 1 ton of wheat straw (wet weight) without considering the contributions of supplementary waste mate- rials, such as wood chips or manure, to ensure comparability. Fig. 2 shows the overall normalised environmental impacts of the five routes to 12 categories [28]. In general, the environmental impacts to the non-toxic categories were relatively low, whereas those to the toxic categories (ET, HTn and HTnc) were relatively high, mainly because the normalisation references of the toxic categories are quite low in the LCIA method (details in the Appendix). The results for these two categories were thus discussed separately. Scenarios S1 and S4 showed similar trends in their normalised impacts, resulting in minor savings to CC and EPf (-0.01 to -0.25 PE), minor loads to AD, EPt and EPm (0.01-0.04 PE) and significant loads to HTnc (6.0-6.8 PE) By contrast, scenarios S3 and S5 resulted in high savings to CC (-0.03 to -0.18 PM (-0.15 to 0.20 PE) and HTc (-0.2 to -0.4 PE) whereas S2 resulted in high savings to EPt and EPm (0.03-0.04 P In terms of overall impact, S3 and S5 outperformed all the other routes. The detailed process contributions to several typical categories (CC, POF, AD, EPt, ET and HTnc) are shown in Fig. 3 in terms of characterised impacts to explain the abovementioned results. The results for the other categories are available in the Appendix.

In S1, the transportation, solid/liquid separation, wastewater treat- ment and composting processes contributed loads to all categories, including CC, POF, AD and EPt, due to fuel and energy consumption. Fig. 3. Process contribution of the five scenarios to the characterized impacts. a) Climate change (CC); b) photochemical ozone formation (POF); c) acidification (AD); d) eutrophication (terrestrial) (EPt); e) ecotoxicity (freshwater) (ET); and f) human toxicity (non-cancer effects) (HTnc).

fertiliser utilisation (approximately -60 kg CO2-eq/t). However, the use-on-land of compost had some impact potentials to AD and EPt due to the loss of N and P to surface water during compost utilisation. Detailed data on use-on-land of compost for the substitution of fertilizer are available in the Appendix. Meanwhile, the impact potentials to ET and HTnc can be ascribed to the presence of trace metal elements in the compost.

In S2, the energy consumption of the smashing, transportation and packaging processes contributed some loads to all impact categories. By contrast, using straw as fodder avoided the impact potentials to CC, POF, AD and EPt by substituting the same amount of silage, but the possible different effects on the livestock were not considered due to lack of data. This ideal assumption may result in uncertainty in the substitution ratio of silage and further affect the impact potentials to certain degrees.

In S3, fuel substitution contributed the largest savings to all impact categories. Specifically, using wheat straw as biomass fuel can avoid significant fossil CO2 emissions (-1330 kg CO2-eq/t) compared with traditional fuels, such as coal. The incineration of residues also resulted
in small savings due to energy recovery and substitution (-16 kg CO2- eq/t). Transportation and smashing contributed some loads to all impact categories due to fuel and energy consumption.

In S4, the incineration of residues contributed some savings to all categories due to the energy recovery from biomass. By contrast, nutrient substitution was beneficial to reducing CC impact (-143 kg CO2-eq/t) due to carbon sequestration and fertiliser avoidance yet resulted in loads to AD and EPt due to nutrient loss and to ET and HTnc due to trace elements. However, these results are uncertain in quanti- fication due to the lack of information about the behaviour of nutrient and metal elements in growing mushroom (the behaviour in compost utilisation was thus considered). Apart from transportation, smashing and fermentation also consumed electricity and heat, thereby contrib- uting slight loads to all categories.

In S5, the produced board, which substituted for boards made from conventional materials, contributed the largest savings to CC (-311 kg CO2-eq/t) and all other impact categories. Additional 15%-25% savings were obtained from the incineration of residues by recovering energy

from the heating value of wheat straw. However, the forming and pressing processes contributed significant loads to all impact categories, counteracting approximately 25% of the non-toxic savings and even 50% of the toxic savings, mainly due to the additive tackiness agent (polymeric diphenyl methane diisocyanate), whose production causes significant impact potentials though the amount is normally low.

Regarding to the climate change impacts, the fuel route (S3) showed the highest saving of 1300 kg CO2-eq/t in all the five scenarios, and the fodder route obtained the lowest saving due to the substitute value of silage. This is in accordance with the results obtained by Soam et al. though the system boundaries are slightly different, which revealed climate change savings of 1471 kg CO2-eq/t and 185 kg CO2-eq/t in for recovering energy and fodder from wheat straw, respectively [29]. The scenarios S1, S4 and S5 also achieved moderate savings to climate change impacts, given the benefits from product substitution. However, fuel or energy recovery from wheat straw is currently an important approach to reduce the climate change impacts as demonstrated by some other studies [30,31], especially under the fossil-based fuel or energy system.
31: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts and process contributions of the five routes </header>

LCA was performed in terms of 1 ton of wheat straw (wet weight) without considering the contributions of supplementary waste mate- rials, such as wood chips or manure, to ensure comparability. Fig. 2 shows the overall normalised environmental impacts of the five routes to 12 categories [28]. In general, the environmental impacts to the non-toxic categories were relatively low, whereas those to the toxic categories (ET, HTn and HTnc) were relatively high, mainly because the normalisation references of the toxic categories are quite low in the LCIA method (details in the Appendix). The results for these two categories were thus discussed separately. The detailed process contributions to several typical categories (CC, POF, AD, EPt, ET and HTnc) are shown in Fig. 3 in terms of characterised impacts to explain the abovementioned results. The results for the other categories are available in the Appendix.

In S1, the transportation, solid/liquid separation, wastewater treat- ment and composting processes contributed loads to all categories, including CC, POF, AD and EPt, due to fuel and energy consumption. Fig. 3. Process contribution of the five scenarios to the characterized impacts. a) Climate change (CC); b) photochemical ozone formation (POF); c) acidification (AD); d) eutrophication (terrestrial) (EPt); e) ecotoxicity (freshwater) (ET); and f) human toxicity (non-cancer effects) (HTnc).

fertiliser utilisation (approximately -60 kg CO2-eq/t). However, the use-on-land of compost had some impact potentials to AD and EPt due to the loss of N and P to surface water during compost utilisation. Detailed data on use-on-land of compost for the substitution of fertilizer are available in the Appendix. Meanwhile, the impact potentials to ET and HTnc can be ascribed to the presence of trace metal elements in the compost.

In S2, the energy consumption of the smashing, transportation and packaging processes contributed some loads to all impact categories. By contrast, using straw as fodder avoided the impact potentials to CC, POF, AD and EPt by substituting the same amount of silage, but the possible different effects on the livestock were not considered due to lack of data. This ideal assumption may result in uncertainty in the substitution ratio of silage and further affect the impact potentials to certain degrees.

In S3, fuel substitution contributed the largest savings to all impact categories. Specifically, using wheat straw as biomass fuel can avoid significant fossil CO2 emissions (-1330 kg CO2-eq/t) compared with traditional fuels, such as coal. The incineration of residues also resulted
in small savings due to energy recovery and substitution (-16 kg CO2- eq/t). Transportation and smashing contributed some loads to all impact categories due to fuel and energy consumption.

In S4, the incineration of residues contributed some savings to all categories due to the energy recovery from biomass. By contrast, nutrient substitution was beneficial to reducing CC impact (-143 kg CO2-eq/t) due to carbon sequestration and fertiliser avoidance yet resulted in loads to AD and EPt due to nutrient loss and to ET and HTnc due to trace elements. However, these results are uncertain in quanti- fication due to the lack of information about the behaviour of nutrient and metal elements in growing mushroom (the behaviour in compost utilisation was thus considered). Apart from transportation, smashing and fermentation also consumed electricity and heat, thereby contrib- uting slight loads to all categories.

In S5, the produced board, which substituted for boards made from conventional materials, contributed the largest savings to CC (-311 kg CO2-eq/t) and all other impact categories. Additional 15%-25% savings were obtained from the incineration of residues by recovering energy

from the heating value of wheat straw. However, the forming and pressing processes contributed significant loads to all impact categories, counteracting approximately 25% of the non-toxic savings and even 50% of the toxic savings, mainly due to the additive tackiness agent (polymeric diphenyl methane diisocyanate), whose production causes significant impact potentials though the amount is normally low.

Regarding to the climate change impacts, the fuel route (S3) showed the highest saving of 1300 kg CO2-eq/t in all the five scenarios, and the fodder route obtained the lowest saving due to the substitute value of silage. This is in accordance with the results obtained by Soam et al. though the system boundaries are slightly different, which revealed climate change savings of 1471 kg CO2-eq/t and 185 kg CO2-eq/t in for recovering energy and fodder from wheat straw, respectively [29]. The scenarios S1, S4 and S5 also achieved moderate savings to climate change impacts, given the benefits from product substitution. However, fuel or energy recovery from wheat straw is currently an important approach to reduce the climate change impacts as demonstrated by some other studies [30,31], especially under the fossil-based fuel or energy system.
32: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts and process contributions of the five routes </header>

LCA was performed in terms of 1 ton of wheat straw (wet weight) without considering the contributions of supplementary waste mate- rials, such as wood chips or manure, to ensure comparability. Fig. 2 shows the overall normalised environmental impacts of the five routes to 12 categories [28]. In general, the environmental impacts to the non-toxic categories were relatively low, whereas those to the toxic categories (ET, HTn and HTnc) were relatively high, mainly because the normalisation references of the toxic categories are quite low in the LCIA method (details in the Appendix). The results for these two categories were thus discussed separately. Scenarios S1 and S4 showed similar trends in their normalised impacts, resulting in minor savings to CC and EPf (-0.01 to -0.25 PE), minor loads to AD, EPt and EPm (0.01-0.04 PE) and significant loads to HTnc (6.0-6.8 PE) By contrast, scenarios S3 and S5 resulted in high savings to CC (-0.03 to -0.18 PM (-0.15 to 0.20 PE) and HTc (-0.2 to -0.4 PE) whereas S2 resulted in high savings to EPt and EPm (0.03-0.04 P In terms of overall impact, S3 and S5 outperformed all the other routes. The detailed process contributions to several typical categories (CC, POF, AD, EPt, ET and HTnc) are shown in Fig. 3 in terms of characterised impacts to explain the abovementioned results. The results for the other categories are available in the Appendix.

In S1, the transportation, solid/liquid separation, wastewater treat- ment and composting processes contributed loads to all categories, including CC, POF, AD and EPt, due to fuel and energy consumption. By contrast, the use-on-land of compost contributed some savings to CC (-162 kg CO2-eq/t) mainly due to the sequestration of undegraded biogenic carbon (approximately -90 kg CO2-eq/t) and the avoidance of

Fig. 3. Process contribution of the five scenarios to the characterized impacts. a) Climate change (CC); b) photochemical ozone formation (POF); c) acidification (AD); d) eutrophication (terrestrial) (EPt); e) ecotoxicity (freshwater) (ET); and f) human toxicity (non-cancer effects) (HTnc).

fertiliser utilisation (approximately -60 kg CO2-eq/t). However, the use-on-land of compost had some impact potentials to AD and EPt due to the loss of N and P to surface water during compost utilisation. Detailed data on use-on-land of compost for the substitution of fertilizer are available in the Appendix. Meanwhile, the impact potentials to ET and HTnc can be ascribed to the presence of trace metal elements in the compost.

In S2, the energy consumption of the smashing, transportation and packaging processes contributed some loads to all impact categories. By contrast, using straw as fodder avoided the impact potentials to CC, POF, AD and EPt by substituting the same amount of silage, but the possible different effects on the livestock were not considered due to lack of data. This ideal assumption may result in uncertainty in the substitution ratio of silage and further affect the impact potentials to certain degrees.

In S3, fuel substitution contributed the largest savings to all impact categories. Specifically, using wheat straw as biomass fuel can avoid significant fossil CO2 emissions (-1330 kg CO2-eq/t) compared with traditional fuels, such as coal. The incineration of residues also resulted
in small savings due to energy recovery and substitution (-16 kg CO2- eq/t). Transportation and smashing contributed some loads to all impact categories due to fuel and energy consumption.

In S4, the incineration of residues contributed some savings to all categories due to the energy recovery from biomass. By contrast, nutrient substitution was beneficial to reducing CC impact (-143 kg CO2-eq/t) due to carbon sequestration and fertiliser avoidance yet resulted in loads to AD and EPt due to nutrient loss and to ET and HTnc due to trace elements. However, these results are uncertain in quanti- fication due to the lack of information about the behaviour of nutrient and metal elements in growing mushroom (the behaviour in compost utilisation was thus considered). Apart from transportation, smashing and fermentation also consumed electricity and heat, thereby contrib- uting slight loads to all categories.

In S5, the produced board, which substituted for boards made from conventional materials, contributed the largest savings to CC (-311 kg CO2-eq/t) and all other impact categories. Additional 15%-25% savings were obtained from the incineration of residues by recovering energy

from the heating value of wheat straw. However, the forming and pressing processes contributed significant loads to all impact categories, counteracting approximately 25% of the non-toxic savings and even 50% of the toxic savings, mainly due to the additive tackiness agent (polymeric diphenyl methane diisocyanate), whose production causes significant impact potentials though the amount is normally low.

Regarding to the climate change impacts, the fuel route (S3) showed the highest saving of 1300 kg CO2-eq/t in all the five scenarios, and the fodder route obtained the lowest saving due to the substitute value of silage. This is in accordance with the results obtained by Soam et al. though the system boundaries are slightly different, which revealed climate change savings of 1471 kg CO2-eq/t and 185 kg CO2-eq/t in for recovering energy and fodder from wheat straw, respectively [29]. The scenarios S1, S4 and S5 also achieved moderate savings to climate change impacts, given the benefits from product substitution. However, fuel or energy recovery from wheat straw is currently an important approach to reduce the climate change impacts as demonstrated by some other studies [30,31], especially under the fossil-based fuel or energy system.
33: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts and process contributions of the five routes </header>

LCA was performed in terms of 1 ton of wheat straw (wet weight) without considering the contributions of supplementary waste mate- rials, such as wood chips or manure, to ensure comparability. Fig. 2 shows the overall normalised environmental impacts of the five routes to 12 categories [28]. In general, the environmental impacts to the non-toxic categories were relatively low, whereas those to the toxic categories (ET, HTn and HTnc) were relatively high, mainly because the normalisation references of the toxic categories are quite low in the LCIA method (details in the Appendix). The results for these two categories were thus discussed separately. Scenarios S1 and S4 showed similar trends in their normalised impacts, resulting in minor savings to CC and EPf (-0.01 to -0.25 PE), minor loads to AD, EPt and EPm (0.01-0.04 PE) and significant loads to HTnc (6.0-6.8 PE) By contrast, scenarios S3 and S5 resulted in high savings to CC (-0.03 to -0.18 PM (-0.15 to 0.20 PE) and HTc (-0.2 to -0.4 PE) whereas S2 resulted in high savings to EPt and EPm (0.03-0.04 P In terms of overall impact, S3 and S5 outperformed all the other routes. The detailed process contributions to several typical categories (CC, POF, AD, EPt, ET and HTnc) are shown in Fig. 3 in terms of characterised impacts to explain the abovementioned results. The results for the other categories are available in the Appendix.

In S1, the transportation, solid/liquid separation, wastewater treat- ment and composting processes contributed loads to all categories, including CC, POF, AD and EPt, due to fuel and energy consumption. By contrast, the use-on-land of compost contributed some savings to CC (-162 kg CO2-eq/t) mainly due to the sequestration of undegraded biogenic carbon (approximately -90 kg CO2-eq/t) and the avoidance of

Fig. 3. Process contribution of the five scenarios to the characterized impacts. a) Climate change (CC); b) photochemical ozone formation (POF); c) acidification (AD); d) eutrophication (terrestrial) (EPt); e) ecotoxicity (freshwater) (ET); and f) human toxicity (non-cancer effects) (HTnc).

fertiliser utilisation (approximately -60 kg CO2-eq/t). However, the use-on-land of compost had some impact potentials to AD and EPt due to the loss of N and P to surface water during compost utilisation. Detailed data on use-on-land of compost for the substitution of fertilizer are available in the Appendix. Meanwhile, the impact potentials to ET and HTnc can be ascribed to the presence of trace metal elements in the compost.

In S2, the energy consumption of the smashing, transportation and packaging processes contributed some loads to all impact categories. By contrast, using straw as fodder avoided the impact potentials to CC, POF, AD and EPt by substituting the same amount of silage, but the possible different effects on the livestock were not considered due to lack of data. This ideal assumption may result in uncertainty in the substitution ratio of silage and further affect the impact potentials to certain degrees.

In S3, fuel substitution contributed the largest savings to all impact categories. Specifically, using wheat straw as biomass fuel can avoid significant fossil CO2 emissions (-1330 kg CO2-eq/t) compared with traditional fuels, such as coal. The incineration of residues also resulted
in small savings due to energy recovery and substitution (-16 kg CO2- eq/t). Transportation and smashing contributed some loads to all impact categories due to fuel and energy consumption.

In S4, the incineration of residues contributed some savings to all categories due to the energy recovery from biomass. By contrast, nutrient substitution was beneficial to reducing CC impact (-143 kg CO2-eq/t) due to carbon sequestration and fertiliser avoidance yet resulted in loads to AD and EPt due to nutrient loss and to ET and HTnc due to trace elements. However, these results are uncertain in quanti- fication due to the lack of information about the behaviour of nutrient and metal elements in growing mushroom (the behaviour in compost utilisation was thus considered). Apart from transportation, smashing and fermentation also consumed electricity and heat, thereby contrib- uting slight loads to all categories.

In S5, the produced board, which substituted for boards made from conventional materials, contributed the largest savings to CC (-311 kg CO2-eq/t) and all other impact categories. Additional 15%-25% savings were obtained from the incineration of residues by recovering energy

from the heating value of wheat straw. However, the forming and pressing processes contributed significant loads to all impact categories, counteracting approximately 25% of the non-toxic savings and even 50% of the toxic savings, mainly due to the additive tackiness agent (polymeric diphenyl methane diisocyanate), whose production causes significant impact potentials though the amount is normally low.

Regarding to the climate change impacts, the fuel route (S3) showed the highest saving of 1300 kg CO2-eq/t in all the five scenarios, and the fodder route obtained the lowest saving due to the substitute value of silage. This is in accordance with the results obtained by Soam et al. though the system boundaries are slightly different, which revealed climate change savings of 1471 kg CO2-eq/t and 185 kg CO2-eq/t in for recovering energy and fodder from wheat straw, respectively [29]. The scenarios S1, S4 and S5 also achieved moderate savings to climate change impacts, given the benefits from product substitution. However, fuel or energy recovery from wheat straw is currently an important approach to reduce the climate change impacts as demonstrated by some other studies [30,31], especially under the fossil-based fuel or energy system.
35: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts and process contributions of the five routes </header>

LCA was performed in terms of 1 ton of wheat straw (wet weight) without considering the contributions of supplementary waste mate- rials, such as wood chips or manure, to ensure comparability. Fig. 2 shows the overall normalised environmental impacts of the five routes to 12 categories [28]. In general, the environmental impacts to the non-toxic categories were relatively low, whereas those to the toxic categories (ET, HTn and HTnc) were relatively high, mainly because the normalisation references of the toxic categories are quite low in the LCIA method (details in the Appendix). The results for these two categories were thus discussed separately. Scenarios S1 and S4 showed similar trends in their normalised impacts, resulting in minor savings to CC and EPf (-0.01 to -0.25 PE), minor loads to AD, EPt and EPm (0.01-0.04 PE) and significant loads to HTnc (6.0-6.8 PE) By contrast, scenarios S3 and S5 resulted in high savings to CC (-0.03 to -0.18 PM (-0.15 to 0.20 PE) and HTc (-0.2 to -0.4 PE) whereas S2 resulted in high savings to EPt and EPm (0.03-0.04 P In terms of overall impact, S3 and S5 outperformed all the other routes. The detailed process contributions to several typical categories (CC, POF, AD, EPt, ET and HTnc) are shown in Fig. 3 in terms of characterised impacts to explain the abovementioned results. The results for the other categories are available in the Appendix.

In S1, the transportation, solid/liquid separation, wastewater treat- ment and composting processes contributed loads to all categories, including CC, POF, AD and EPt, due to fuel and energy consumption. By contrast, the use-on-land of compost contributed some savings to CC (-162 kg CO2-eq/t) mainly due to the sequestration of undegraded biogenic carbon (approximately -90 kg CO2-eq/t) and the avoidance of
