Fig. 3. Process contribution of the five scenarios to the characterized impacts. a) Climate change (CC); b) photochemical ozone formation (POF); c) acidification (AD); d) eutrophication (terrestrial) (EPt); e) ecotoxicity (freshwater) (ET); and f) human toxicity (non-cancer effects) (HTnc).

fertiliser utilisation (approximately -60 kg CO2-eq/t). However, the use-on-land of compost had some impact potentials to AD and EPt due to the loss of N and P to surface water during compost utilisation. Detailed data on use-on-land of compost for the substitution of fertilizer are available in the Appendix. Meanwhile, the impact potentials to ET and HTnc can be ascribed to the presence of trace metal elements in the compost.

In S2, the energy consumption of the smashing, transportation and packaging processes contributed some loads to all impact categories. By contrast, using straw as fodder avoided the impact potentials to CC, POF, AD and EPt by substituting the same amount of silage, but the possible different effects on the livestock were not considered due to lack of data. This ideal assumption may result in uncertainty in the substitution ratio of silage and further affect the impact potentials to certain degrees.

In S3, fuel substitution contributed the largest savings to all impact categories. Specifically, using wheat straw as biomass fuel can avoid significant fossil CO2 emissions (-1330 kg CO2-eq/t) compared with traditional fuels, such as coal. The incineration of residues also resulted
in small savings due to energy recovery and substitution (-16 kg CO2- eq/t). Transportation and smashing contributed some loads to all impact categories due to fuel and energy consumption.

In S4, the incineration of residues contributed some savings to all categories due to the energy recovery from biomass. By contrast, nutrient substitution was beneficial to reducing CC impact (-143 kg CO2-eq/t) due to carbon sequestration and fertiliser avoidance yet resulted in loads to AD and EPt due to nutrient loss and to ET and HTnc due to trace elements. However, these results are uncertain in quanti- fication due to the lack of information about the behaviour of nutrient and metal elements in growing mushroom (the behaviour in compost utilisation was thus considered). Apart from transportation, smashing and fermentation also consumed electricity and heat, thereby contrib- uting slight loads to all categories.

In S5, the produced board, which substituted for boards made from conventional materials, contributed the largest savings to CC (-311 kg CO2-eq/t) and all other impact categories. Additional 15%-25% savings were obtained from the incineration of residues by recovering energy

from the heating value of wheat straw. However, the forming and pressing processes contributed significant loads to all impact categories, counteracting approximately 25% of the non-toxic savings and even 50% of the toxic savings, mainly due to the additive tackiness agent (polymeric diphenyl methane diisocyanate), whose production causes significant impact potentials though the amount is normally low.

Regarding to the climate change impacts, the fuel route (S3) showed the highest saving of 1300 kg CO2-eq/t in all the five scenarios, and the fodder route obtained the lowest saving due to the substitute value of silage. This is in accordance with the results obtained by Soam et al. though the system boundaries are slightly different, which revealed climate change savings of 1471 kg CO2-eq/t and 185 kg CO2-eq/t in for recovering energy and fodder from wheat straw, respectively [29]. The scenarios S1, S4 and S5 also achieved moderate savings to climate change impacts, given the benefits from product substitution. However, fuel or energy recovery from wheat straw is currently an important approach to reduce the climate change impacts as demonstrated by some other studies [30,31], especially under the fossil-based fuel or energy system.
36: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts and process contributions of the five routes </header>

LCA was performed in terms of 1 ton of wheat straw (wet weight) without considering the contributions of supplementary waste mate- rials, such as wood chips or manure, to ensure comparability. Fig. 2 shows the overall normalised environmental impacts of the five routes to 12 categories [28]. In general, the environmental impacts to the non-toxic categories were relatively low, whereas those to the toxic categories (ET, HTn and HTnc) were relatively high, mainly because the normalisation references of the toxic categories are quite low in the LCIA method (details in the Appendix). The results for these two categories were thus discussed separately. Scenarios S1 and S4 showed similar trends in their normalised impacts, resulting in minor savings to CC and EPf (-0.01 to -0.25 PE), minor loads to AD, EPt and EPm (0.01-0.04 PE) and significant loads to HTnc (6.0-6.8 PE) By contrast, scenarios S3 and S5 resulted in high savings to CC (-0.03 to -0.18 PM (-0.15 to 0.20 PE) and HTc (-0.2 to -0.4 PE) whereas S2 resulted in high savings to EPt and EPm (0.03-0.04 P In terms of overall impact, S3 and S5 outperformed all the other routes. The detailed process contributions to several typical categories (CC, POF, AD, EPt, ET and HTnc) are shown in Fig. 3 in terms of characterised impacts to explain the abovementioned results. The results for the other categories are available in the Appendix.

In S1, the transportation, solid/liquid separation, wastewater treat- ment and composting processes contributed loads to all categories, including CC, POF, AD and EPt, due to fuel and energy consumption. By contrast, the use-on-land of compost contributed some savings to CC (-162 kg CO2-eq/t) mainly due to the sequestration of undegraded biogenic carbon (approximately -90 kg CO2-eq/t) and the avoidance of

37: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts and process contributions of the five routes </header>

LCA was performed in terms of 1 ton of wheat straw (wet weight) without considering the contributions of supplementary waste mate- rials, such as wood chips or manure, to ensure comparability. Fig. 2 shows the overall normalised environmental impacts of the five routes to 12 categories [28]. In general, the environmental impacts to the non-toxic categories were relatively low, whereas those to the toxic categories (ET, HTn and HTnc) were relatively high, mainly because the normalisation references of the toxic categories are quite low in the LCIA method (details in the Appendix). The results for these two categories were thus discussed separately. Scenarios S1 and S4 showed similar trends in their normalised impacts, resulting in minor savings to CC and EPf (-0.01 to -0.25 PE), minor loads to AD, EPt and EPm (0.01-0.04 PE) and significant loads to HTnc (6.0-6.8 PE) By contrast, scenarios S3 and S5 resulted in high savings to CC (-0.03 to -0.18 PM (-0.15 to 0.20 PE) and HTc (-0.2 to -0.4 PE) whereas S2 resulted in high savings to EPt and EPm (0.03-0.04 P In terms of overall impact, S3 and S5 outperformed all the other routes. The detailed process contributions to several typical categories (CC, POF, AD, EPt, ET and HTnc) are shown in Fig. 3 in terms of characterised impacts to explain the abovementioned results. The results for the other categories are available in the Appendix.

In S1, the transportation, solid/liquid separation, wastewater treat- ment and composting processes contributed loads to all categories, including CC, POF, AD and EPt, due to fuel and energy consumption. Fig. 3. Process contribution of the five scenarios to the characterized impacts. a) Climate change (CC); b) photochemical ozone formation (POF); c) acidification (AD); d) eutrophication (terrestrial) (EPt); e) ecotoxicity (freshwater) (ET); and f) human toxicity (non-cancer effects) (HTnc).

However, the use-on-land of compost had some impact potentials to AD and EPt due to the loss of N and P to surface water during compost utilisation. Detailed data on use-on-land of compost for the substitution of fertilizer are available in the Appendix. Meanwhile, the impact potentials to ET and HTnc can be ascribed to the presence of trace metal elements in the compost.

In S2, the energy consumption of the smashing, transportation and packaging processes contributed some loads to all impact categories. By contrast, using straw as fodder avoided the impact potentials to CC, POF, AD and EPt by substituting the same amount of silage, but the possible different effects on the livestock were not considered due to lack of data. This ideal assumption may result in uncertainty in the substitution ratio of silage and further affect the impact potentials to certain degrees.

In S3, fuel substitution contributed the largest savings to all impact categories. Specifically, using wheat straw as biomass fuel can avoid significant fossil CO2 emissions (-1330 kg CO2-eq/t) compared with traditional fuels, such as coal. The incineration of residues also resulted
in small savings due to energy recovery and substitution (-16 kg CO2- eq/t). Transportation and smashing contributed some loads to all impact categories due to fuel and energy consumption.

In S4, the incineration of residues contributed some savings to all categories due to the energy recovery from biomass. By contrast, nutrient substitution was beneficial to reducing CC impact (-143 kg CO2-eq/t) due to carbon sequestration and fertiliser avoidance yet resulted in loads to AD and EPt due to nutrient loss and to ET and HTnc due to trace elements. However, these results are uncertain in quanti- fication due to the lack of information about the behaviour of nutrient and metal elements in growing mushroom (the behaviour in compost utilisation was thus considered). Apart from transportation, smashing and fermentation also consumed electricity and heat, thereby contrib- uting slight loads to all categories.

In S5, the produced board, which substituted for boards made from conventional materials, contributed the largest savings to CC (-311 kg CO2-eq/t) and all other impact categories. Additional 15%-25% savings were obtained from the incineration of residues by recovering energy

from the heating value of wheat straw. However, the forming and pressing processes contributed significant loads to all impact categories, counteracting approximately 25% of the non-toxic savings and even 50% of the toxic savings, mainly due to the additive tackiness agent (polymeric diphenyl methane diisocyanate), whose production causes significant impact potentials though the amount is normally low.

Regarding to the climate change impacts, the fuel route (S3) showed the highest saving of 1300 kg CO2-eq/t in all the five scenarios, and the fodder route obtained the lowest saving due to the substitute value of silage. This is in accordance with the results obtained by Soam et al. though the system boundaries are slightly different, which revealed climate change savings of 1471 kg CO2-eq/t and 185 kg CO2-eq/t in for recovering energy and fodder from wheat straw, respectively [29]. The scenarios S1, S4 and S5 also achieved moderate savings to climate change impacts, given the benefits from product substitution. However, fuel or energy recovery from wheat straw is currently an important approach to reduce the climate change impacts as demonstrated by some other studies [30,31], especially under the fossil-based fuel or energy system.
38: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts and process contributions of the five routes </header>

LCA was performed in terms of 1 ton of wheat straw (wet weight) without considering the contributions of supplementary waste mate- rials, such as wood chips or manure, to ensure comparability. Fig. 2 shows the overall normalised environmental impacts of the five routes to 12 categories [28]. In general, the environmental impacts to the non-toxic categories were relatively low, whereas those to the toxic categories (ET, HTn and HTnc) were relatively high, mainly because the normalisation references of the toxic categories are quite low in the LCIA method (details in the Appendix). The results for these two categories were thus discussed separately. The detailed process contributions to several typical categories (CC, POF, AD, EPt, ET and HTnc) are shown in Fig. 3 in terms of characterised impacts to explain the abovementioned results. The results for the other categories are available in the Appendix.

In S1, the transportation, solid/liquid separation, wastewater treat- ment and composting processes contributed loads to all categories, including CC, POF, AD and EPt, due to fuel and energy consumption. Fig. 3. Process contribution of the five scenarios to the characterized impacts. a) Climate change (CC); b) photochemical ozone formation (POF); c) acidification (AD); d) eutrophication (terrestrial) (EPt); e) ecotoxicity (freshwater) (ET); and f) human toxicity (non-cancer effects) (HTnc).

fertiliser utilisation (approximately -60 kg CO2-eq/t). However, the use-on-land of compost had some impact potentials to AD and EPt due to the loss of N and P to surface water during compost utilisation. Detailed data on use-on-land of compost for the substitution of fertilizer are available in the Appendix. Meanwhile, the impact potentials to ET and HTnc can be ascribed to the presence of trace metal elements in the compost.

In S2, the energy consumption of the smashing, transportation and packaging processes contributed some loads to all impact categories. By contrast, using straw as fodder avoided the impact potentials to CC, POF, AD and EPt by substituting the same amount of silage, but the possible different effects on the livestock were not considered due to lack of data. This ideal assumption may result in uncertainty in the substitution ratio of silage and further affect the impact potentials to certain degrees.

In S3, fuel substitution contributed the largest savings to all impact categories. Specifically, using wheat straw as biomass fuel can avoid significant fossil CO2 emissions (-1330 kg CO2-eq/t) compared with traditional fuels, such as coal. The incineration of residues also resulted
in small savings due to energy recovery and substitution (-16 kg CO2- eq/t). Transportation and smashing contributed some loads to all impact categories due to fuel and energy consumption.

In S4, the incineration of residues contributed some savings to all categories due to the energy recovery from biomass. By contrast, nutrient substitution was beneficial to reducing CC impact (-143 kg CO2-eq/t) due to carbon sequestration and fertiliser avoidance yet resulted in loads to AD and EPt due to nutrient loss and to ET and HTnc due to trace elements. However, these results are uncertain in quanti- fication due to the lack of information about the behaviour of nutrient and metal elements in growing mushroom (the behaviour in compost utilisation was thus considered). Apart from transportation, smashing and fermentation also consumed electricity and heat, thereby contrib- uting slight loads to all categories.

In S5, the produced board, which substituted for boards made from conventional materials, contributed the largest savings to CC (-311 kg CO2-eq/t) and all other impact categories. Additional 15%-25% savings were obtained from the incineration of residues by recovering energy

from the heating value of wheat straw. However, the forming and pressing processes contributed significant loads to all impact categories, counteracting approximately 25% of the non-toxic savings and even 50% of the toxic savings, mainly due to the additive tackiness agent (polymeric diphenyl methane diisocyanate), whose production causes significant impact potentials though the amount is normally low.

Regarding to the climate change impacts, the fuel route (S3) showed the highest saving of 1300 kg CO2-eq/t in all the five scenarios, and the fodder route obtained the lowest saving due to the substitute value of silage. This is in accordance with the results obtained by Soam et al. though the system boundaries are slightly different, which revealed climate change savings of 1471 kg CO2-eq/t and 185 kg CO2-eq/t in for recovering energy and fodder from wheat straw, respectively [29]. The scenarios S1, S4 and S5 also achieved moderate savings to climate change impacts, given the benefits from product substitution. However, fuel or energy recovery from wheat straw is currently an important approach to reduce the climate change impacts as demonstrated by some other studies [30,31], especially under the fossil-based fuel or energy system.
39: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts and process contributions of the five routes </header>

LCA was performed in terms of 1 ton of wheat straw (wet weight) without considering the contributions of supplementary waste mate- rials, such as wood chips or manure, to ensure comparability. Fig. 2 shows the overall normalised environmental impacts of the five routes to 12 categories [28]. In general, the environmental impacts to the non-toxic categories were relatively low, whereas those to the toxic categories (ET, HTn and HTnc) were relatively high, mainly because the normalisation references of the toxic categories are quite low in the LCIA method (details in the Appendix). The results for these two categories were thus discussed separately. Scenarios S1 and S4 showed similar trends in their normalised impacts, resulting in minor savings to CC and EPf (-0.01 to -0.25 PE), minor loads to AD, EPt and EPm (0.01-0.04 PE) and significant loads to HTnc (6.0-6.8 PE) By contrast, scenarios S3 and S5 resulted in high savings to CC (-0.03 to -0.18 PM (-0.15 to 0.20 PE) and HTc (-0.2 to -0.4 PE) whereas S2 resulted in high savings to EPt and EPm (0.03-0.04 P In terms of overall impact, S3 and S5 outperformed all the other routes. The detailed process contributions to several typical categories (CC, POF, AD, EPt, ET and HTnc) are shown in Fig. 3 in terms of characterised impacts to explain the abovementioned results. The results for the other categories are available in the Appendix.

In S1, the transportation, solid/liquid separation, wastewater treat- ment and composting processes contributed loads to all categories, including CC, POF, AD and EPt, due to fuel and energy consumption. By contrast, the use-on-land of compost contributed some savings to CC (-162 kg CO2-eq/t) mainly due to the sequestration of undegraded biogenic carbon (approximately -90 kg CO2-eq/t) and the avoidance of

Fig. 3. Process contribution of the five scenarios to the characterized impacts. a) Climate change (CC); b) photochemical ozone formation (POF); c) acidification (AD); d) eutrophication (terrestrial) (EPt); e) ecotoxicity (freshwater) (ET); and f) human toxicity (non-cancer effects) (HTnc).

fertiliser utilisation (approximately -60 kg CO2-eq/t). However, the use-on-land of compost had some impact potentials to AD and EPt due to the loss of N and P to surface water during compost utilisation. Detailed data on use-on-land of compost for the substitution of fertilizer are available in the Appendix. Meanwhile, the impact potentials to ET and HTnc can be ascribed to the presence of trace metal elements in the compost.

In S2, the energy consumption of the smashing, transportation and packaging processes contributed some loads to all impact categories. By contrast, using straw as fodder avoided the impact potentials to CC, POF, AD and EPt by substituting the same amount of silage, but the possible different effects on the livestock were not considered due to lack of data. This ideal assumption may result in uncertainty in the substitution ratio of silage and further affect the impact potentials to certain degrees.

In S3, fuel substitution contributed the largest savings to all impact categories. Specifically, using wheat straw as biomass fuel can avoid significant fossil CO2 emissions (-1330 kg CO2-eq/t) compared with traditional fuels, such as coal. The incineration of residues also resulted
in small savings due to energy recovery and substitution (-16 kg CO2- eq/t). Transportation and smashing contributed some loads to all impact categories due to fuel and energy consumption.

In S4, the incineration of residues contributed some savings to all categories due to the energy recovery from biomass. By contrast, nutrient substitution was beneficial to reducing CC impact (-143 kg CO2-eq/t) due to carbon sequestration and fertiliser avoidance yet resulted in loads to AD and EPt due to nutrient loss and to ET and HTnc due to trace elements. However, these results are uncertain in quanti- fication due to the lack of information about the behaviour of nutrient and metal elements in growing mushroom (the behaviour in compost utilisation was thus considered). Apart from transportation, smashing and fermentation also consumed electricity and heat, thereby contrib- uting slight loads to all categories.

In S5, the produced board, which substituted for boards made from conventional materials, contributed the largest savings to CC (-311 kg CO2-eq/t) and all other impact categories. Additional 15%-25% savings were obtained from the incineration of residues by recovering energy

from the heating value of wheat straw. However, the forming and pressing processes contributed significant loads to all impact categories, counteracting approximately 25% of the non-toxic savings and even 50% of the toxic savings, mainly due to the additive tackiness agent (polymeric diphenyl methane diisocyanate), whose production causes significant impact potentials though the amount is normally low.

Regarding to the climate change impacts, the fuel route (S3) showed the highest saving of 1300 kg CO2-eq/t in all the five scenarios, and the fodder route obtained the lowest saving due to the substitute value of silage. This is in accordance with the results obtained by Soam et al. though the system boundaries are slightly different, which revealed climate change savings of 1471 kg CO2-eq/t and 185 kg CO2-eq/t in for recovering energy and fodder from wheat straw, respectively [29]. The scenarios S1, S4 and S5 also achieved moderate savings to climate change impacts, given the benefits from product substitution. However, fuel or energy recovery from wheat straw is currently an important approach to reduce the climate change impacts as demonstrated by some other studies [30,31], especially under the fossil-based fuel or energy system.
40: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts and process contributions of the five routes </header>

LCA was performed in terms of 1 ton of wheat straw (wet weight) without considering the contributions of supplementary waste mate- rials, such as wood chips or manure, to ensure comparability. Fig. 2 shows the overall normalised environmental impacts of the five routes to 12 categories [28]. In general, the environmental impacts to the non-toxic categories were relatively low, whereas those to the toxic categories (ET, HTn and HTnc) were relatively high, mainly because the normalisation references of the toxic categories are quite low in the LCIA method (details in the Appendix). The results for these two categories were thus discussed separately. Scenarios S1 and S4 showed similar trends in their normalised impacts, resulting in minor savings to CC and EPf (-0.01 to -0.25 PE), minor loads to AD, EPt and EPm (0.01-0.04 PE) and significant loads to HTnc (6.0-6.8 PE) By contrast, scenarios S3 and S5 resulted in high savings to CC (-0.03 to -0.18 PM (-0.15 to 0.20 PE) and HTc (-0.2 to -0.4 PE) whereas S2 resulted in high savings to EPt and EPm (0.03-0.04 P In terms of overall impact, S3 and S5 outperformed all the other routes. The detailed process contributions to several typical categories (CC, POF, AD, EPt, ET and HTnc) are shown in Fig. 3 in terms of characterised impacts to explain the abovementioned results. The results for the other categories are available in the Appendix.

In S1, the transportation, solid/liquid separation, wastewater treat- ment and composting processes contributed loads to all categories, including CC, POF, AD and EPt, due to fuel and energy consumption. By contrast, the use-on-land of compost contributed some savings to CC (-162 kg CO2-eq/t) mainly due to the sequestration of undegraded biogenic carbon (approximately -90 kg CO2-eq/t) and the avoidance of

41: The five routes for straw utilisation presented various environmental impacts to different categories, with several parameters controlling their performance. A sensitivity analysis was thus performed to identify the sensitive parameters for each route, and the improvement potentials of each route were further investigated by optimising these parameters.

The key operational parameters for each route, including energy and material consumption, and the substitution ratios of products were selected for the sensitivity analysis. Fig. 4 shows the relative sensitivity ratios of the selected parameters to climate change impacts, which were normalised into percentages of the highest sensitivity ratio in each route. The relative sensitivity ratios can only be compared within the same scenario. Positive values indicate that increasing the corresponding parameters can contribute some savings to climate change impacts, and vice versa. The results for the other impact categories, which can be found in the Appendix, indicate that the sensitivity ratio may vary ac- cording to the impact categories due to their different contributions. For the climate change impacts (Fig. 4), the substitution ratios of the products were identified as dominant sensitive parameters in relevant scenarios (S2, S3 and S5). This is because of the significant contribution of the product substitution to the savings in climate change impacts. The energy consumption in S1, S2, S3 and S4 and the additive consumption in S5 were also influential to varying degrees with negative values. This phenomenon implies that a slight reduction in these energy and material consumptions may result in a remarkable improvement in mitigation of climate change impacts. However, as mentioned earlier, the substitution ratio of silage was uncertain and was assigned as an ideal condition, and the substitution ratios of fossil fuel and board were determined

according to the heating value and material properties, respectively. Therefore, these parameters did not show reliable potentials for optimisation.

A 10% reduction to the parameters of energy and material con- sumption was assumed for the optimisation of all routes. The improve- ment potentials of each route are listed in Table 2 as improvement percentages in terms of the initial impacts. S1 consumed 213 MJ/t heat during the solid/liquid separation after anaerobic digestion and 59.5 kWh/t electricity during the composting of digestate. Saving 10% of the energy consumption can contribute additional 5.26%, 7.72%, 6.66% and 5.73% savings to CC, PM, HTc and POF, respectively, and marginal improvements to the other impact categories. By contrast, S2 consumed 15 kWh/t and 7.5 kWh/t in the smashing and packaging processes, and the optimisation by 10% can result in 95% and 53.5% higher savings to CC and POF, respectively. These results can be mainly ascribed to the fact that the initial CC and POF impacts were relatively low. Therefore, small amounts of reduction resulted in high improvement ratios. In S3 and S4, the energy consumed in the smashing and processing processes showed only marginal contributions to improvement, but the potentials from improving product application warrant further investigation. S5 can contribute significant improvements to OD, EPm, ET, HT and the other impact categories by reducing 10% of the electricity consumption in drying/smashing process and the additive consumption in the form- ing process. In sum, S5 shows the biggest room for improvement amongst all routes even though both S3 and S5 demonstrate a favour- able performance in terms of their overall environmental impacts. These improvement spaces are theoretical, and the actual reduction of the electricity consumption needs practical verification.

according to the heating value and material properties, respectively. Therefore, these parameters did not show reliable potentials for optimisation.

A 10% reduction to the parameters of energy and material con- sumption was assumed for the optimisation of all routes. The improve- ment potentials of each route are listed in Table 2 as improvement percentages in terms of the initial impacts. S1 consumed 213 MJ/t heat during the solid/liquid separation after anaerobic digestion and 59.5 kWh/t electricity during the composting of digestate. Saving 10% of the energy consumption can contribute additional 5.26%, 7.72%, 6.66% and 5.73% savings to CC, PM, HTc and POF, respectively, and marginal improvements to the other impact categories. By contrast, S2 consumed 15 kWh/t and 7.5 kWh/t in the smashing and packaging processes, and the optimisation by 10% can result in 95% and 53.5% higher savings to CC and POF, respectively. These results can be mainly ascribed to the fact that the initial CC and POF impacts were relatively low. Therefore, small amounts of reduction resulted in high improvement ratios. In S3 and S4, the energy consumed in the smashing and processing processes showed only marginal contributions to improvement, but the potentials from improving product application warrant further investigation. S5 can contribute significant improvements to OD, EPm, ET, HT and the other impact categories by reducing 10% of the electricity consumption in drying/smashing process and the additive consumption in the form- ing process. In sum, S5 shows the biggest room for improvement amongst all routes even though both S3 and S5 demonstrate a favour- able performance in terms of their overall environmental impacts. These improvement spaces are theoretical, and the actual reduction of the electricity consumption needs practical verification.
43: The five routes for straw utilisation presented various environmental impacts to different categories, with several parameters controlling their performance. A sensitivity analysis was thus performed to identify the sensitive parameters for each route, and the improvement potentials of each route were further investigated by optimising these parameters.

The key operational parameters for each route, including energy and material consumption, and the substitution ratios of products were selected for the sensitivity analysis. Fig. 4 shows the relative sensitivity ratios of the selected parameters to climate change impacts, which were normalised into percentages of the highest sensitivity ratio in each route. The relative sensitivity ratios can only be compared within the same scenario. Positive values indicate that increasing the corresponding parameters can contribute some savings to climate change impacts, and vice versa. The results for the other impact categories, which can be found in the Appendix, indicate that the sensitivity ratio may vary ac- cording to the impact categories due to their different contributions. For the climate change impacts (Fig. 4), the substitution ratios of the products were identified as dominant sensitive parameters in relevant scenarios (S2, S3 and S5). This is because of the significant contribution of the product substitution to the savings in climate change impacts. The energy consumption in S1, S2, S3 and S4 and the additive consumption in S5 were also influential to varying degrees with negative values. This phenomenon implies that a slight reduction in these energy and material consumptions may result in a remarkable improvement in mitigation of climate change impacts. However, as mentioned earlier, the substitution ratio of silage was uncertain and was assigned as an ideal condition, and the substitution ratios of fossil fuel and board were determined

46: To reveal their potential environmental performance, five techno- logical routes for straw utilisation were investigated via LCA modelling using operational data from full-scale practices. S1 co-digested and co- composted wheat straw with large amounts of sewage and manure, respectively, which resulted in low contributions of wheat straw to the liquid fertiliser and compost products. The fuel and raw material routes (S3 and S5) also used certain amounts of waste wood, and wheat straw significantly contributed to their products in terms of heating value and material quality, respectively. Using full-scale input and output data, the material flow analysis revealed that the transfer coefficients from the wheat straw to the targeted products ranged from 93% to 100% in terms of TS across all routes.

On the basis of the material flows and the practical data on material and energy consumptions, a comprehensive LCA was performed in terms of processing 1 t of wheat straw, and the fuel route producing briquette fuel (S3) and the raw material route producing board (S5) achieved lower overall environmental impacts in 12 categories compared with the other routes. The fuel and board substitution in fuel and raw material routes (S3 and S5), respectively, contributed significant amounts of savings to climate change (-1330 kg CO2-eq/t and -311 kg CO2-eq/t, respectively). By contrast, the compost and nutrient utilisation in the fertilizer and basic material routes (S1 and S4) also avoided impacts to climate change, but at the same time contributed loads to acidification and eutrophication (0.01-0.04 PE/t), and human toxicity (non-cancer effects) due to the runoff of nutrient and trace elements. The additive tackiness agent in the raw material route (S5) and the energy con- sumption in all five routes showed sensitivity to climate change and other impacts. Varying degrees of improvement in environmental per- formance can be achieved for all the five routes even by slightly reducing the material and energy consumption. Whilst this study quantitatively compared the environmental performance and improve- ment potentials of these five routes, the uncertainty of specific tech- nologies and local conditions should be further investigated during the application of these routes. In particular, the scenario sensitivity ana- lyses on the composition, collection, location of the crop straw, as well
